sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I can see the character has moved LEFT. The character is now on the LEFT side of the green frame. The feet are on the LEFT side (in the 180-degree orientation, feet are on TOP, but the character is rotated 180 degrees, so the feet are actually on the LEFT side of the image).
Wait, no. In the 180-degree orientation, the character is upside down. The feet are on TOP (relative to the character's orientation), which means they're at the top of the character's bounding box. But the character is rotated 180 degrees, so the feet are actually at the bottom of the character's bounding box in the image.
Actually, looking at the image, I see the red feet are on the LEFT side of the character. This is the 180-degree orientation, where the character is upside down. The "P" shape is now an upside-down "P", which looks like a "q" or "d" shape.
Let me check the green count.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_total = sum(n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N')
red_total = sum(n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'R')
gray_total = sum(n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G')
print(f"Green: {green_total}, Red: {red_total}, Gray: {gray_total}")

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        minr = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxr = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        minc = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxc = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={minr}-{maxr} cols={minc}-{maxc}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Actually, the white body is 15 columns wide, and the green frame is 9 columns wide. To fully cover the green frame horizontally, the white body's left edge should be at cols 41 - k, where 0 <= k <= 6 (so that the white body's right edge is at cols 41 - k + 14 = 55 - k, which is >= 49 when k <= 6).
